topic: kerning pet peeves — badly spaced logos in public

turns.content: i need to vent about something. i was walking through the station today and i saw this huge banner for a new shopping complex and the kerning on the logo was SO bad. like the letters were just floating in space with no relationship to each other. how does something like that get approved

The fact that it made it from someone's screen to a massive printed banner without anyone catching it is honestly baffling. But it happens constantly — bad kerning at scale is one of those things that most people can't articulate but subconsciously feel is "off."

What was the worst part — uneven spacing throughout, or was it a specific letter pair that was especially egregious?

it was a mix of roman letters and katakana and the spacing between the two scripts was just... chaotic. like the latin characters had normal tracking but the katakana was spaced way too tight and then there was this huge gap between the two parts of the name. zero visual rhythm. it looked like two different fonts that accidentally ended up next to each other

Bilingual kerning is genuinely hard to get right, but that's exactly why it requires a designer who actually understands both scripts. The problem you're describing — Latin characters at one rhythm and katakana at a completely different one — usually happens when someone sets each part of the name independently and then just shoves them together without optically adjusting the spacing.

Katakana naturally has more internal white space than Latin letterforms because of how the characters are constructed. If you don't compensate for that — tightening the katakana slightly or loosening the Latin to match — you get exactly that disjointed effect.

And the gap between the two parts? That's the dead giveaway that nobody looked at the whole thing as a single unit. They treated it as two separate text blocks instead of one integrated wordmark.

Do you see this kind of thing a lot around Osaka?

constantly. like once you start noticing bad kerning you can never unsee it. it's a curse honestly. my friends think i'm insane because i'll stop in the middle of a conversation to point at a sign and go "look at the space between the T and the A, that's criminal"

The keming curse is real — once your eye is trained, every poorly spaced sign, menu, and billboard is an assault. Your friends don't see it because they process the word as a whole, but you see the individual spaces between each letterform.

The T-A pair is one of the worst offenders too. It's like the poster child for kerning problems because the A tucks under the T's crossbar so naturally — when a font or a designer doesn't account for that optical space, it looks like T  A with a canyon between them.

Other classic nightmare pairs:
- **V and A** — the angled strokes create a huge optical gap
- **L and T** next to round letters — the overshoot is always wrong
- **W and anything** — W is just a kerning disaster in most typefaces

The worst is when you see these issues on something expensive and permanent — like carved into a building facade or etched into glass. That's not a typo you can fix with an update.

oh god permanent signage with bad kerning is the worst. there's a dental clinic near my apartment that has the most atrocious letter spacing carved into stone above the entrance and every time i walk past it i physically wince. it's been there for years. it's going to be there forever

Carved. Into. Stone. That's typographic trauma that outlasts the designer, the client, and possibly the building.

Somewhere, a sign maker got that file, looked at the spacing, probably thought "hmm" for about half a second, and then just went ahead and carved it. Or worse — the designer sent it exactly like that because they never learned to optically kern.

The thing that kills me about these situations is that kerning takes maybe an extra 15-20 minutes to get right on a wordmark. That's it. For something that's going to be carved into stone and displayed for decades, you'd think someone would spend those 15 minutes. But no.

Do you ever feel the urge to leave an anonymous note? "Dear dental clinic, your kerning is causing me more pain than any root canal."

lmao i've literally thought about it. or like sliding my business card under the door with a note that says "i can fix your sign" but that feels unhinged. also the sign is carved into stone so i can't actually fix it anyway. the only solution is demolition

"The only solution is demolition" should be on a t-shirt for designers. Or at least a sticker you can slap on offending signage.

The guerrilla business card approach is honestly not the worst idea though — not for the stone sign, that's beyond saving, but for businesses with vinyl or printed signage. Some small business owners genuinely don't know their signage is bad and would appreciate someone offering to clean it up. It's a weird way to get clients but it's happened.

What's the most egregious example you've seen in the wild? Like the absolute worst kerning crime that still haunts you?

okay this one still kills me. there was a wedding venue — a WEDDING venue — that had the word "FOREVER" on their main sign and the spacing was so bad it read like "FOR EVER" with this massive gap after the R. like the one word that absolutely needs to feel connected and unified and they split it in half. the irony was so thick i almost cried

"FOR EVER" on a wedding venue is genuinely poetic in the worst way. The one word in the English language that's supposed to convey unbroken continuity, and the kerning literally broke it apart. You couldn't write better satire.

The R-E pair after a tight FOR is a classic problem — the R's leg creates this optical gap that needs to be manually tightened. But on a wedding venue sign? Someone should have caught that. The client, the designer, the printer, the installer — it passed through multiple pairs of eyes and nobody said "hey, does that look like two words to you?"

This is honestly why I think basic typography should be taught more widely. Not to make everyone a type designer, but so that when someone spends money on a sign for their business — especially one that's supposed to evoke emotion — at least one person in the room can say "the spacing is off."

exactly. and it's not like kerning is some obscure esoteric skill. it's literally just "do these letters look evenly spaced." you don't need a design degree for that, you just need eyes and like five minutes of attention. honestly the world would be a more beautiful place if people just looked at their own signage for five minutes before approving it

Five minutes and a squint. That's literally the kerning test — squint at the text until the letters blur and see if the gray value looks even. If there are obvious light or dark spots, the spacing is off. Anyone can do it.

But you're fighting against a world where most people think of text as content, not as a visual element. They read the word, the word says the right thing, job done. The space between the letters is invisible to them.

Which is honestly why designers exist and why this stuff matters — you're the person who sees what everyone else feels but can't name. When a sign looks "cheap" or "off" to a regular person, nine times out of ten it's the spacing, and they just don't know that's what's bothering them.

Keep wincing at the dental clinic. Keep noticing the gaps. The world needs people who care about the space between the letters.

honestly this conversation made me feel seen lol. nobody in my life understands why i get physically upset about letter spacing. they think i'm being dramatic but it's like... this is literally what i do. it's like asking a chef not to notice when food is badly seasoned

The chef analogy is perfect. Nobody calls a chef dramatic for noticing that a dish is undersalted at a restaurant. It's the same professional awareness — you've trained your eye to see what untrained eyes can't, and you can't just switch it off because you've left the studio.

It's not dramatic, it's occupational. You see the world through a typographic lens and that lens doesn't have an off switch. The dental clinic sign, the wedding venue, the train station banner — they're all just poorly seasoned dishes to you.

At least you can channel that frustration into making sure your own work is impeccable. Every time you manually kern a wordmark, you're one less bad sign in the world. That's a public service, honestly.
